title: Making a plywood medicine cabinet with a door
subtitle: Build a wall-mounted box cabinet with a hinged door from 18 mm plywood
excerpt: How to cut, assemble, and hang a small wall-mounted plywood cabinet with a butt-hinged door, covering the box construction, door fitting, magnetic catch, and wall-fixing method.
sourceNotes: City and Guilds Level 2 Site Carpentry. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics. TRADA Wood Information Sheet (plywood joinery).

glossaryTerms.definition: A shallow recess chiselled into the door edge and door frame so that the hinge leaf sits flush with the surrounding timber surface when closed. Housing the hinge prevents it from standing proud, which would create a gap at the closing edge. The housing depth equals the thickness of one hinge leaf; mark it with a marking gauge set to this depth.

content.text: A small wall cabinet, 400 mm wide × 600 mm tall × 150 mm deep, uses roughly half a sheet of 18 mm plywood. The box is assembled with screwed butt joints; the door is hung on two 50 mm butt hinges with
so it closes flush with the front face. A magnetic catch holds it shut. The finished cabinet can be painted or veneered to match the room.

18 mm birch or MDF-faced plywood for all panels. Cutting list for the 400 × 600 × 150 mm cabinet: two side panels 600 × 150 mm; top and bottom panels 400 × 150 mm (these sit between the sides); back panel 400 × 600 mm from 6 mm ply or hardboard; door panel 400 × 600 mm from 18 mm ply. Two 50 mm butt hinges. One magnetic catch. 40 mm No. 8 countersunk screws. PVA wood glue.

Cut all panels to size with the circular saw and a straightedge guide. Check each panel for square before assembly, any out-of-square panel will transfer the error through the whole box. Sand all cut edges to 120 grit.

Apply PVA glue to the top and bottom edges of each side panel. Position the top and bottom panels between the sides. Drill pilot holes from the outside of the side panels into the top and bottom panels, countersink, and drive 40 mm screws. Check the box is square by measuring diagonals: both diagonals should be equal. If one diagonal is longer, clamp across that diagonal to pull the box square before the glue sets. Glue and pin the back panel in place.

Cut the door panel 1 mm narrower than the cabinet opening on the hinge side and 2 mm narrower on the closing side to allow clearance. Hold the door in the opening and mark the hinge positions at 100 mm from each top and bottom corner. Set the marking gauge to the thickness of one hinge leaf and mark the housing depth on the door edge and the cabinet side. Chop out the housing with a sharp chisel, working from the marked lines inward. Fit the hinges, checking the door closes flush with the cabinet front face. Adjust the housings if it does not.
Step 4, fit the catch and hang the cabinet

Fit a magnetic catch to the inside of the cabinet at the closing side, and the catch plate to the inner face of the door, aligning them so the door closes flat. Fix the cabinet to the wall: mark the wall through two fixing holes drilled in the back panel or through a hanging rail fixed inside the top of the cabinet. Drill and plug the wall, check the cabinet is level with the spirit level, and screw home.

attrs.symptom: Door binds at the top or bottom corner when closing
attrs.cause: The hinge housing on one leaf is too shallow, causing the hinge to stand proud and preventing the door from closing fully.
attrs.fix: Open the door and check which leaf is proud. Deepen the housing by a fraction with the chisel, test, and repeat until the door closes without binding.

attrs.symptom: Cabinet is square when assembled but racks out of square when hung on the wall
attrs.cause: The wall is out of plumb and the fixing points have pulled one side of the cabinet slightly forward.
attrs.fix: Pack the fixing point that is pulling the cabinet out of plumb with a thin shim between the cabinet back and the wall until the door closes correctly.
